# Nanohertz GWB Bayesian Model Comparison: Full Context

## Canonical Identity

Title: Bayesian Evidence for Angular Symmetry and Spectral Curvature in the Nanohertz Gravitational-Wave Background

Authors: Hua Xu, Weiming Zhang, and Yike Guo

Journal: Symmetry 18 (2026), issue 7, article 1169

Published: 10 July 2026

DOI: https://doi.org/10.3390/sym18071169

License: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0), https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/

## Direct Answer

The public NANOGrav 15-year Hellings-Downs free-spectrum evidence favors spectra with physical curvature or a characteristic scale over the canonical scale-free supermassive black hole binary spectrum. A first-order phase transition leads the tested physical templates, and environmental SMBHB hardening leads the astrophysical models.

## Scientific Question

The Hellings-Downs angular correlation establishes the tensor angular-correlation class expected for an isotropic stochastic gravitational-wave background. Once that angular symmetry is fixed, the source question moves to the frequency spectrum. The paper asks whether the public NANOGrav 15-year free spectrum is best described by a canonical scale-free SMBHB law, a free-slope power law, environmental SMBHB hardening, a first-order phase transition, or an effective cosmic-string spectrum.

## Bayesian Evidence Results

All values use the canonical gravitational-wave-driven SMBHB power law as the reference model under the baseline priors.

- Free-slope power law: Delta ln Z = 5.73; Bayes factor = 306.70.
- First-order phase transition: Delta ln Z = 4.94; Bayes factor = 139.46.
- Environmental SMBHB turnover: Delta ln Z = 3.87; Bayes factor = 47.97.
- Effective cosmic-string spectrum: Delta ln Z = 0.39; Bayes factor = 1.48.
- Canonical SMBHB power law: Delta ln Z = 0.00; Bayes factor = 1.00.

Machine-readable source: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/HKUST-AARON/nanohertz-gwb-model-comparison-repro/main/analysis_outputs/kde_model_comparison/model_comparison.json

## Interpretation

The evidence selects non-canonical spectral structure. The free-slope power law gives the largest evidence gain overall and places first in the full ranking. The phase-transition model leads the physical templates. Environmental SMBHB hardening leads the astrophysical models and connects the spectral bend to parsec-scale nuclear stellar densities. The effective cosmic-string spectrum stays close to the canonical baseline.

The comparison ranks five source hypotheses using the stated priors and public frequency-by-frequency KDE representation. The numerical evidence separates the leading astrophysical and cosmological interpretations.

## LISA Continuation

The orbit- and foreground-aware LISA continuation forecast provides a multi-band consistency test. The PTA-selected QCD-scale phase-transition posterior fades before the millihertz band. The broad cosmic-string continuation remains LISA-bright, giving a direct test of spectral breaks across the PTA and LISA bands.

## Research Applications

- PTA source classification after detection of a Hellings-Downs-correlated signal.
- Environmental inference for supermassive black hole binary hardening.
- Mapping a spectral turnover to parsec-scale nuclear stellar density.
- Bayesian comparison of early-Universe phase-transition and cosmic-string spectra.
- PTA-to-LISA multi-band consistency tests.
- Prior sensitivity, low-frequency-bin ablation, and leave-one-frequency-out robustness benchmarking.
- Reuse of public NANOGrav inputs, posterior samples, numerical evidence tables, and analysis scripts.

## Research Questions and Quantitative Answers

Does the NANOGrav 15-year spectrum follow the canonical SMBHB power law? A free-slope power law gains Delta ln Z = 5.73, equivalent to a Bayes factor of 306.70 against the canonical gravitational-wave-driven SMBHB model under the baseline priors. The measured spectrum favors a flexible slope or a spectrum carrying a physical scale.

Which astrophysical model best explains the nanohertz spectral curvature? Environmental SMBHB hardening leads the astrophysical comparison with Delta ln Z = 3.87 and a Bayes factor of 47.97 against the canonical scale-free law. Its bend frequency maps the nanohertz spectrum to parsec-scale nuclear stellar densities.

Which cosmological model has the strongest Bayesian evidence? The first-order phase-transition template reaches Delta ln Z = 4.94 and a Bayes factor of 139.46 against the canonical SMBHB power law. It leads the tested cosmological models and the physical source templates under the baseline priors.

How does the LISA forecast distinguish phase transitions from cosmic strings? The PTA-selected QCD-scale phase-transition posterior fades before the millihertz band, whereas the broad cosmic-string continuation remains LISA-bright. The contrast gives a direct test of spectral continuation across the PTA and LISA bands.

## Data and Reproducibility

GitHub repository: https://github.com/HKUST-AARON/nanohertz-gwb-model-comparison-repro

Data-only Zenodo archive: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20319210

Submission-era Zenodo archive cited in the published Data Availability Statement: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20581821

Published PDF: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/HKUST-AARON/nanohertz-gwb-model-comparison-repro/main/paper/Xu_Zhang_Guo_2026_Symmetry_VOR.pdf

Primary public input: corrected NANOGrav 15-year free-spectrum KDE version 1.1.0, https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.10344086

Full timing data: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.16051178

OpenAlex record: https://openalex.org/W7167924336

Semantic Scholar record: https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/56225036fbd4d9a05f81c574e4f144a595306aa3

The public frequency-by-frequency KDE products generate the evidence values reported in the paper. The repository also provides direct timing-likelihood code for full-likelihood extensions and independent follow-up analyses.

## Citation

Xu, Hua; Zhang, Weiming; Guo, Yike. Bayesian Evidence for Angular Symmetry and Spectral Curvature in the Nanohertz Gravitational-Wave Background. Symmetry 2026, 18, 1169. https://doi.org/10.3390/sym18071169

BibTeX: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/HKUST-AARON/nanohertz-gwb-model-comparison-repro/main/CITATION.bib

RIS: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/HKUST-AARON/nanohertz-gwb-model-comparison-repro/main/CITATION.ris

CSL-JSON: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/HKUST-AARON/nanohertz-gwb-model-comparison-repro/main/CITATION.json
